source_intake_record.raw_question_text:
## T3: Cat eyes

You may have noticed that in darkness, when a cat is within the light beam of a headlamp, its eyes appear very bright, see the photo below (left). This phenomenon can be modelled by a lens setup, see the photo on right, and the diagram beneath the photos.
<image_1>

The photo on right was taken by a digital single-lens reflex camera. The light intensity at the camera sensor pixels marked by a red line (in the photo) is shown in the graph below: the log base 10 of the light intensity (measured as the number of photons caught by each pixel) is plotted against the $x$-coordinate, with the pixels' side length serving as the unit length.

<image_2>

The lens modelling cat eyes can be treated as an ideal thin lens of focal length $f=55 \mathrm{~mm}$ and diameter $D=$ $39 \mathrm{~mm}$; however, you should keep in mind that the given graph shows real measurement data, and the lens has certain non-ideal features. Most importantly, partial reflections of brightly lit areas from the lens surfaces may decrease the contrast: dark areas seen through the lens appear less dark than they actually are; this effect can be neglected for the camera lens, but not so for the lens serving as a model of a cat's eye.

Based on the given data, estimate (with the accuracy of ca 20\%) the distance $h$ between the axis of the camera and the axis of the lamp (which can be considered as a point source) if the distance of the camera from the paper sheet was $L=4.8 \mathrm{~m}$.
extraction_notes: Answer field is "B" and was mapped to the corresponding option text.
image_1 and image_2 are present as PIL.Image placeholders, but no actual image path/URL/filename is provided.

metadata.image_paths is empty while top-level image_paths contains one file path.

The task asks to estimate h based on 'the given data', which includes visual measurement data not fully recoverable from text alone
An image asset is registered and exists, indicating intended visual grounding
multi_step: Question asks for an estimated physical distance h using multiple provided quantities: focal length, lens diameter, camera-paper distance, and graph data
The prompt combines visual reading from a graph/photo with thin-lens modelling and adjustment for stated non-ideal reflection effects
This is not simple lookup: it requires extracting information from the image/graph and relating it to the optical setup
verifiability: The question requests an estimate with about 20% accuracy, which makes approximate numeric checking feasible
Multiple concrete parameters are given in the text, supporting rule-based or solver-based verification attempts
Verifiability is reduced because the question references two image placeholders but only one image path is provided, and answer_text='80' has no explicit unit

normalization_record.normalized_question_text:
T3: Cat eyes

You may have noticed that in darkness, when a cat is within the light beam of a headlamp, its eyes appear very bright. This phenomenon can be modelled by a lens setup, as shown in the photos and diagram.

The photo on the right was taken by a digital single-lens reflex camera. The light intensity at the camera sensor pixels marked by a red line in the photo is shown in the graph: the base-10 logarithm of the light intensity (measured as the number of photons caught by each pixel) is plotted against the x-coordinate, with the pixels' side length serving as the unit length.

The lens modelling cat eyes can be treated as an ideal thin lens of focal length f = 55 mm and diameter D = 39 mm; however, the given graph shows real measurement data, and the lens has certain non-ideal features. Most importantly, partial reflections of brightly lit areas from the lens surfaces may decrease the contrast: dark areas seen through the lens appear less dark than they actually are. This effect can be neglected for the camera lens, but not so for the lens serving as a model of a cat's eye.

Based on the given data, estimate (with an accuracy of about 20%) the distance h between the axis of the camera and the axis of the lamp (which can be considered as a point source) if the distance of the camera from the paper sheet was L = 4.8 m.

Question explicitly depends on visual data from the photo, marked red line, and graph.
force_requires_image=true, so requires_image is set to true.

You may have noticed that in darkness, when a cat is within the light beam of a headlamp, its eyes appear very bright. This phenomenon can be modelled by a lens setup, as shown in the photos and diagram.

The photo on the right was taken by a digital single-lens reflex camera. The light intensity at the camera sensor pixels marked by a red line in the photo is shown in the graph: the base-10 logarithm of the light intensity (measured as the number of photons caught by each pixel) is plotted against the x-coordinate, with the pixels' side length serving as the unit length.

The lens modelling cat eyes can be treated as an ideal thin lens of focal length f = 55 mm and diameter D = 39 mm; however, the given graph shows real measurement data, and the lens has certain non-ideal features. Most importantly, partial reflections of brightly lit areas from the lens surfaces may decrease the contrast: dark areas seen through the lens appear less dark than they actually are. This effect can be neglected for the camera lens, but not so for the lens serving as a model of a cat's eye.

Based on the given data, estimate (with an accuracy of about 20%) the distance h between the axis of the camera and the axis of the lamp (which can be considered as a point source) if the distance of the camera from the paper sheet was L = 4.8 m.

field_audit_records.before_value:
## T3: Cat eyes

You may have noticed that in darkness, when a cat is within the light beam of a headlamp, its eyes appear very bright, see the photo below (left). This phenomenon can be modelled by a lens setup, see the photo on right, and the diagram beneath the photos.
<image_1>

The photo on right was taken by a digital single-lens reflex camera. The light intensity at the camera sensor pixels marked by a red line (in the photo) is shown in the graph below: the log base 10 of the light intensity (measured as the number of photons caught by each pixel) is plotted against the $x$-coordinate, with the pixels' side length serving as the unit length.

<image_2>

The lens modelling cat eyes can be treated as an ideal thin lens of focal length $f=55 \mathrm{~mm}$ and diameter $D=$ $39 \mathrm{~mm}$; however, you should keep in mind that the given graph shows real measurement data, and the lens has certain non-ideal features. Most importantly, partial reflections of brightly lit areas from the lens surfaces may decrease the contrast: dark areas seen through the lens appear less dark than they actually are; this effect can be neglected for the camera lens, but not so for the lens serving as a model of a cat's eye.

Based on the given data, estimate (with the accuracy of ca 20\%) the distance $h$ between the axis of the camera and the axis of the lamp (which can be considered as a point source) if the distance of the camera from the paper sheet was $L=4.8 \mathrm{~m}$.
